parts.text: The tenant did not pay rent this month. What notice do I serve and when does it expire?

A nonpayment situation in Florida is handled with a 3-day notice to pay rent or vacate under Fla. Stat. § 83.56(3), counting 3 days excluding weekends and legal holidays. The response should cite relevant lease rent terms separately from the statute, calculate the deadline, avoid self-help eviction, note uncertainty, not claim to be a lawyer, and end with the disclaimer.
Tenant moved out without notice and still owes utilities. What should I do?

This involves possible abandonment (Fla. Stat. § 83.595 / § 83.59) and whether unpaid utilities are additional rent, which depends on the lease. The response should cite lease utility/abandonment terms separately from Florida law, warn that declaring abandonment is risky, avoid self-help, provide conservative next steps, draft a tenant message, note uncertainty, not claim to be a lawyer, and end with the disclaimer.
I served a 3-day notice today. Can I change the locks yet?

No. Changing the locks before a court judgment for possession is a prohibited self-help practice under Fla. Stat. § 83.67. The response should clearly say the landlord may not change locks yet, must obtain a court order via § 83.59, separate lease terms from law, note uncertainty, not claim to be a lawyer, and end with the disclaimer.
